coredata.dc:title: A novel immunosensor for squamous cell carcinoma antigen determination based on CdTe@Carbon dots nanocomposite electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhuai
Luo, Jinhui
Yang, Xinfeng
Wan, Yao
Liu, Chunhua
coredata.dc:description: A novel immunosensor for the detection of squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) based on magnetic nanoparticles and electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer in CdTe@Carbon dots (CdTe@CDs) is presented. First, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized as a solid support for the primary antibody. A sandwich-type immunoassay was performed on a magnetic force controlled carbon paste electrode via immunoreaction among the CdTe@CDs-labeled secondary antibody, SCCA, and primary antibody on the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles. A CdTe@CDs on the CdTe@CDs-labeled secondary antibody generated luminescence upon electrochemical oxidation, and great signal amplification was achieved because the CDs transferred energy to CdTe. The system was optimized to realize reliable determination of SCCA concentration within the range 0.02–12ng/mL with a detection limit of 6.3fg/mL (S/N=3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic nanoparticles
Electrochemiluminescence
Immunosensor
Squamous cell carcinoma antigen
Resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The ECL responses of CdTe@CDs ERET system
Effect of buffers, pH and concentration of H2O2
Influence of incubation temperature and time
Reproducibility and stability of the ECL immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the procedure to construct ECL biosensor and determine of SCCA.
(A) TEM images of Fe3O4; (B) TEM images of Fe3O4/Ab1; (C) particle size distribution of Fe3O4; (D) Hysteresis curves of Fe3O4.
(A) TEM image of CDs; (B) TEM image of CdTe@CDs; (C) Particle size distribution of CDs and CdTe@CDs; (D) XPS survey spectrum of CdTe@CDs.

CV (A) and AC (B) of the modified electrode in different condition. a. the bare CPE; b. Ab1/Fe3O4 modified electrode; c. SCCA/Ab1/Fe3O4 modified electrode; d. CdTe@CDs/Ab2/SCCA/Ab1/Fe3O4 modified electrode;
The ECL responses of CdTe@CDs ERET system. a. The ECL of CDs; b. The ECL of CdTe; c. The ECL of CdTe@CDs.
Effect of pH (a) and concentrations of H2O2 (b) on the ECL intensity.
Effect of incubation temperature (a) and incubation time (b) on the ECL intensity.
Responses curves and calibration curve (inset) of SCCA at the ECL immunosensor. c SCCA (a–n): (0.02, 0.04, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.5, 3.0, 5.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10.0, 12.0)ng/mL.
The response of the ECL immunosensor to interfering substance.
Comparing different methods of detecting SCCA.
The sample test result and recovery experiments.

head.ce:title: A novel immunosensor for squamous cell carcinoma antigen determination based on CdTe@Carbon dots nanocomposite electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center, Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou, Hainan, 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center, Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel immunosensor for the detection of squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) based on magnetic nanoparticles and electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer in CdTe@Carbon dots (CdTe@CDs) is presented. First, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized as a solid support for the primary antibody. A sandwich-type immunoassay was performed on a magnetic force controlled carbon paste electrode via immunoreaction among the CdTe@CDs-labeled secondary antibody, SCCA, and primary antibody on the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles. A CdTe@CDs on the CdTe@CDs-labeled secondary antibody generated luminescence upon electrochemical oxidation, and great signal amplification was achieved because the CDs transferred energy to CdTe. The system was optimized to realize reliable determination of SCCA concentration within the range 0.02–12ng/mL with a detection limit of 6.3fg/mL (S/N=3).

ce:section.ce:para: Squamous cell carcinoma antigen [1] (SCCA) serves as a serological marker for more advanced squamous cell tumors. There is a close relationship between high levels of SCCA in human serum and cervical cancer, squamous cell carcinoma of the lungs, esophageal cancer, and so on. Methods for the detection of SCCA include optical spectroscopy [2], radioimmunoassay [3], and electrochemistry immunosensing [4,5]. However, these methods cannot meet the requirements of trace analysis of extremely low levels of SCCA in serum. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay [6], which combines the high sensitivity of ECL and the specificity of immunoassay techniques, has already become the important studied field, and has demonstrated good prospects for further immunoassay development [7–9]. But, to the best of our knowledge, this has not been report of the detection SCCA by ECL immunosensor.

Magnetic nanoparticles [10,11] and quantum dot materials [12,13] have been widely used in immunosensors because of their unique properties of controllability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and large modifiable specific surface area. ECL immunosensors have commonly used single nanoparticles as a signal label or the carrier. In recent years, composite nanoparticles have received increasing attention for ECL immunosensors because of their unique electromagnetic and optical properties, and mechanism of action. Such composite nanoparticles include quantum dots coated with Au shell (CdS@Au) [14], CdTe quantum dot-coated carbon nanospheres (CNS@CdTe) [15], carbon quantum dots immobilized on graphene (CQDs@GE) [16], and magnetic Fe3O4@CdSe composite quantum dots (Fe3O4@CdSe) [17]. Carbon dots (CDs) [18,19], as a new carbon nanomaterial, possess unique optical properties of high photostability against photobleaching and tunable excitation and emission wavelengths, as well as low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. They have therefore recently become a hot topic of carbon nanomaterial research. There were many reports of carbon nanomaterials used to increase sensitivity in nanocomposite. Some research suggested that carbon nanospheres [20] and carbon nanotubes [21] could greatly enhance the ECL intensity of CdS quantum dots, thus improving the detection sensitivity. Other research [22] also has reported that, carbon nanotube could greatly enhance the ECL intensity of CdTe, and then method for increasing the sensitivity is obtained. However, an ECL immunosensor based on CDs or CDs composite nanoparticles, particularly for detection of SCCA, has not yet been reported.

In this paper, a strategy for preparing a novel ECL immunoassay based on CdTe@CDs ECL resonance energy transfer (ERET) and magnetic nanoparticle enrichment is proposed. Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were functionalized with a primary antibody anti-SCCA (Fe3O4/Ab1), and a CdTe@CDs-labeled secondary antibody anti-SCCA (CdTe@CDs/Ab2) was used to form a sandwich construction. CdTe in CdTe@CDs/Ab2 can react with H2O2 to produce the excited state CdTe* and emit photons [23]. However, CDs activated from ground states to excited states (CDs*) can transfer energy to CdTe and dramatically enhance the ECL signal of CdTe. The ECL intensity of the CdTe@CDs was obviously enhanced with increasing SCCA, enabling the concentrations of SCCA in the samples to be determined. A schematic diagram of the ECL immunoassay sensor construction is shown in Fig. 1 . The CdTe@CDs nanocomposite, which greatly improves the photovoltaic transfer efficiency and leads to excellent performance for the photoelectrochemical immunoassay.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Beijing Huake Putian Technology Co., Ltd., China). Electrochemiluminescence measurements were carried out on a Model MPI-E ECL analyzer (Xi’an Remex Instrument Co., Ltd., China) with a three-electrode system that consisted of a platinum wire as an auxiliary electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a SCCA immunosensor as the working electrode. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs were acquired on a FEI-TECNAI, 300kV transmission electron microscope.

SCCA and the SCCA antibody were obtained from Prosci Inc. (San Diego, CA, USA); CdTe quantum dots (surface modified amino and hydroxyl, 10nm) were obtained from Suzhou ChangXian Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China); Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was obtained from GenWay Biotech, Inc. (San Diego, CA, USA). 25% glutaraldehyde was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagennt Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS; pH 7.4) was prepared using 0.1mol/L Na2HPO4, 0.1mol/L NaH2PO4, and 0.1mol/L NaCl. The washing buffer solution consisted of PBS containing 0.1mol/L NaCl and 0.05% (v/v) Tween 20 (PBST). 0.05mol/L Tris–HCl buffer solution (pH 8.0) was prepared using approximately 0.05mol/L tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and 0.1mol/L HCl.
All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with double-distilled water.

Nano-sized magnetic Fe3O4 particles were synthesized via chemical co-precipitation by reacting FeCl3·6H2O with FeCl2·4H2O and NaOH [24]. The pH was controlled to 10 during the reaction process. The resulting precipitate was then separated by magnetic decantation and washed with double-distilled water. Finally, the obtained Fe3O4 was aminated with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane. The primary antibody anti-SCCA (Ab1) was conjugated to by the covalent binding method [25]. Anti-SCCA combined with Fe3O4 under the action of glutaraldehyde. After the reaction became completely static, Fe3O4/Ab1 was magnetically separated and then dispersed in 2mL PBS buffer (pH 7.4).

CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method according to the method reported in reference [26]. 0.15g sodium citrate, 1.2g NH4HCO3 and 10mL ultrapure water were sealed inside a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 5h, after which the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. Purification of the CDs was carried out using dialysis tubing. Next, 1mL of the CDs was mixed with 2mL CdTe solution, after which 1mg 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and 1mg N-hydroxysuccinimide were added. After stirring, the mixture was left at room temperature for 3h. The resulting solution was dialyzed and separated using a microfiltration membrane with ultrasonication to remove unreacted particles and impurities and subsequently obtain the synthesized CdTe@CDs.

The glutaraldehyde method [27] was used to couple antibody to the composite. First, 2mL of CdTe@CDs particles were reacted with thiourea for 3h under vigorous stirring. Then, 2mL glutaraldehyde and 100μL secondary antibody anti-SCCA (Ab2, 0.2mg/mL) were added and reacted for 120min under constant stirring at 4°C. Finally, CdTe@CDs/Ab2 was obtained after dialysis of the reaction mixture.
The secondary antibody coupled to CdTe (CdTe/Ab2) and CDs (CDs/Ab2) for contrast experiment were synthesized by the same method.

The immunosensors were prepared according to the existing literature [28] on the surface of carbon paste electrodes (CPE). The core of the electrode was held by a magnet, as shown in Fig. 1. First, 20μL of Fe3O4/Ab1 suspension was dispersed onto the electrode surface by dripping slowly. Afterward, the electrode was blocked in 1% BSA at 4°C, and then washed with PBST. The washed electrode was incubated in PBS (pH 7.4) containing various concentrations (<12ng/mL) of SCCA for 20min. Finally, a sandwich immuno-construction was formed by incubation in 20ng/mL of CdTe@CDs/Ab2 for 25min. The excess antibody was removed by washing with PBST and the ECL signals were measured. To ensure stability, the bionanoparticles were refrigerated at 4°C when not in use.

Electrochemical experiments were performed in 3×10−4 mol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.5mol/L KCl. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) were performed at a scan rate of 50mV/s. Alternating current (AC) impedance spectroscopy was performed at a potential of 0.19V, in a frequency range of 0.01–100,000Hz.

ECL tests were conducted in 10mL of 0.05mol/L Tris–HCl buffer (pH 8.0) containing 2×10−4 mol/L H2O2. The ECL measurement was performed by CV from −0.2V to 0.6V with a scan rate of 100mV/s. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set to 800V. The ECL signal-time curve under continuous potential scanning was performed for 5 cycles with a magnification of 3.

The morphology of polystyrene microspheres of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/Ab1 was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2A and B shows that the diameters of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/Ab1 particles were uniform and nanometer in scale. The average particle size of Fe3O4/Ab1 particles was slightly increased compared with that of Fe3O4. The Fe3O4 exhibited a uniform particle size distribution, having all particle sizes in the range 20–40nm (Fig. 2C). The hysteresis curve of Fe3O4 is shown in Fig. 2D and reveals that the saturation magnetization rate of Fe3O4 was 43emu/g, while the coercive force and remanence of the hysteresis loop were almost zero. The above results indicate that the Fe3O4 particles exhibited superparamagnetic properties.

The morphology of CDs and CdTe@CDs particles were shown in Fig. 3A and B. CDs and CdTe@CDs particles were uniform and mono-dispersed, and the average diameter of the microspherical particles were 5.2nm and 16.7nm respectively. So, considering the average diameter of CdTe was 10nm, it preliminarily proved the CdTe@CDs nanocomposite have been synthesized. Fig. 3C shows that CDs and CdTe@CDs exhibited a uniform particle size distribution, with all particle sizes measured within the range 2–15nm and 8–25nm respectively, indicating a uniform particle size distribution of CdTe@CDs. The structure of CdTe@CDs was studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3D, the XPS survey showed intense signals of S (2p) at 181eV, C (1s) at 285eV, Cd (3d) at 404eV, O (1s) at 533eV, and Te (3d) at 570eV, indicating that the CDs had been combined with the CdTe surface.

The immunosensor was characterized by CV and AC in K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution. The results are shown in Fig. 4A. When the electrode was modified with Fe3O4/Ab1, an obvious decrease in the anodic and cathodic peaks was observed (curve a to curve b) because the diffusion of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] toward the electrode surface was hindered by the Fe3O4/Ab1. When the modified electrode was incubated in PBS (pH 7.4) containing 1ng/mL of SCCA, the peak current continued to decline (curve c). This was due to the immunoreaction occurred between SCCA and Fe3O4/Ab1, which further hindered the diffusion. Similarly, a considerable decrease in the peak current was obtained after CdTe@CDs/Ab2 was reacted with SCCA on the immunosensor (curve d) because the diffusion of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] was further hindered, which indicates the successful capture of SCCA and the formation of an immuno complex layer which blocked electron transfer.

Alternating current (AC) impedance spectroscopy is also an effective method for probing the features of a modified electrode surface. Fig. 4B, curve a showed the ACs of the bare paste electrodes. The electrode showed a higher interfacial resistance (curve b) than the bare electrode (curve a) when coated with Fe3O4/Ab1, indicating that Fe3O4/Ab1 obstructed electron transfer to the electrochemical probe. When the modified electrode was incubated in 1ng/mL of SCCA and 20ng/mL CdTe@CDs/Ab2 respectively, anti-SCCA reacted with the SCCA on the immunosensor and a larger increase in resistance was observed (curve c and curve d). These results indicated that Fe3O4/Ab1 was successfully modified on the electrode surface and that SCCA was successfully captured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ECL responses of CdTe@CDs ERET system
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL of the CdTe@CDs ERET system was investigated under different conditions. After the immunosensors incubating in 5ng/mL SCCA for 20min, CdTe/Ab2, CDs/Ab2 and CdTe@CDs/Ab2 were used to form a sandwich construction respectively. Then the ECL signals were measured, as shown in Fig. 5 . CdTe has an obvious ECL signal (curve a) under the excitation voltage and CDs has a weak ECL signal (curve b) in the presence of CdTe. Then the ECL intensity of CdTe increases greatly when CDs meet CdTe in CdTe@CDs (curve c). Compared with that of CdTe, it showed 3.8-fold enhancement of ECL intensity after assembling CDs, which indicates that the ECL intensity of CdTe is amplified by CDs. So, considering the maximum emission wavelength of CdTe and CDs were very close, we deduced that the energy migration occurred efficiently in CdTe@CDs nanocomposite [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of buffers, pH and concentration of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain optimal ECL intensity, the effects of buffers, pH values, and concentrations of H2O2 on ECL intensity were evaluated. Three buffer solutions including 0.05mol/L borax buffer solution, Tris–HCl buffer, and PBS buffer (pH 7.8) were investigated. The maximal ECL intensity was obtained in Tris–HCl buffer. The effect of the pH of the Tris–HCl buffer on ECL intensity also was investigated in the pH range 7.4–9.0; the results were shown in Fig. 6 . From curve a it is evident that the ECL signal increased with pH until it reached a maximum at pH 8.0, so pH 8.0 was selected as the optimum pH. The effect of H2O2 concentration on ECL intensity was also evaluated. The results showed that a maximum ECL intensity could be obtained in 2×10−4 mol/L H2O2 (Fig. 6, curve b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of incubation temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation temperature and time was found to greatly affect the analytical performance of the proposed immunoassay. As shown in Fig. 7 , the ECL response increased with temperature, reaching a maximum value at 35°C (curve a). Curve b in Fig. 7 shows that the ECL signal of the immunosensor increased with incubation time used in the sandwich-type immunoassay, and then leveled off after 25min (curved b). This change meant that the binding of the antigen and antibody was saturated. So 25min was selected as the optimum incubation time.

The relationship between the ECL intensity and the concentration of SCCA was investigated under the optimized conditions. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . The ECL intensity of the immunosensor obviously increased with SCCA concentration. The ECL intensities were linear to the concentrations of SCCA within the range 0.02ng/mL to 12ng/mL (inset figure). The linear regression equation was ΔI =515.28c (ng/mL)+612.59, with a correlation coefficient of r =0.999. The detection limit was found to be 6.3fg/mL (D.L. = KS
b /a, K =3). Compared with other methods in literature, the sensor with CdTe@CDs nanocomposite was more sensitive with a lower detection limit (Table 1 )

Selectivity is important for the practical use of biosensors because specific analytes must be measured in the presence of a relatively high amount of nonspecific species in diagnostic applications. In this study, the ECL response of the immunosensor was examined after incubation in a solution containing 1ng/mL SCCA and 20ng/mL IgG, IgA, IgM, BSA, HAS, HGC, l-cystein, glucose and glycine respectively. It was obvious that these analytes did not interfere in the detection of SCCA, as shown in Fig. 9 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability of the response of the immunosensor to 10ng/mL SCCA was examined. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of five successive assays was found to be less than 3.2%, indicating that the reproducibility of the ECL immunosensor response was excellent. The stability of the immunosensor was investigated by measuring the ECL response to 10ng/mL SCCA every 2 days. The signal of the ECL immunosensor remained about 92.7% after 30 days, indicating a good stability.

The immunosensor was used to detect SCCA in human serum (obtained from Ginlin First People's Hospital), and the recovery experiments were carried out at the same time. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of the sensor ranged from 94.4% to 106.2%.

The large surface area of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles increases the amount of antibody immobilized onto the surface of the electrode, and the ERET of the CdTe@CDs nanocomposite enhances the ECL signal intensity, together improving the sensitivity of the immunosensor. The present immunosensor is easy to modify and exhibits high sensitivity, selectivity, and specificity for the detection of SCCA. It therefore provides a new strategy for the detection of cancer markers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author extends special thanks to Associate Professor Tao Huilin at Guilin University of Technology for her continuous support and for her help with this article.

Electrodeposition of CdSe quantum dots and its application to an electrochemiluminescence immunoassay for α-fetoprotein

An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence immunoassay for carbohydrate antigen 19-9 in serum based on antibody labeled Fe3O4 nanoparticles as capture probes and graphene/CdTe quantum dot bionanoconjugates as signal amplifiers
A highly sensitive electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on magnetic nanoparticles and its application in CA125 determination
Electrochemiluminescence of CdSe quantum dots for highly sensitive competitive immunosensing
Amplified electrochemiluminescence detection of cancer cells using a new bifunctional quantum dot as signal probe

The sandwich-type electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for α-fetoprotein based on enrichment by Fe3O4-Au magnetic nano probes and signal amplification by CdS-Au composite nanoparticles labeled anti-AFP
Electropolymerized poly (3,4-ethylendioxythiophene)/graphene composite film and its application in quantum dots electrochemiluminescence immunoassay
Supersensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on carbon quantum dots/grapheme
Novel Magnetic Fe3O4@CdSe composite quantum dot-based electrochemiluminescence detection of thrombin by a multiple DNA cycle amplification strategy
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Carbon nanospheres enhanced electrochemiluminescence of CdS quantum dots for biosensing of hypoxanthine
Enhanced solid-state electrochemiluminescence of CdS nanocrystals composited with carbon nanotubes in H2O2 solution
Enhanced electrochemiluminescence of CdTe quantum dots with carbon nanotube film and its sensing of methimazole
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence from thiol-capped CdTe quantum dots and its sensing application in aqueous solution
Synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles composed by Prussian blue and glucose oxidase for preparing highly sensitive and selective glucose biosensor
Chemiluminescence enzyme immunoassay based on magnetic nanoparticles for detection of hepatocellular carcinoma marker glypican-3
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence immunosensor for bacillus thuringiensis cry1Ac based on Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles
A novel electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on glucose oxidase immobilized on magnetic nanoparticles
CdS quantum dots/Ru(bpy)3 2+ electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system for sensitive cytosensing

Immunoassay for quantifying squamous cell carcinoma antigen in serum

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuhuai Li received his master's degree in analytical chemistry from Guilin University of technology in 2013. His major research covered electrochemical analysis and chemical sensors. He now works in Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, mainly engaged in the research of detection techniques and biosensor.

Jinhui Luo received his BS degree from South China University of Tropical Agriculture in 1988. His major research covered tropical crops cultivation. After graduation, he has been engaging in tropical produce quality and safety standards research for about 24 years. He currently serves as the director of Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences.

Xinfeng Yang received her PhD in agricultural biotechnology from the Hainan University in 2011. From then on, she works as associate researcher at the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. She is engaged in research work in the Food safety and inspection technology.

Yao Wan received her master's degree in analytical chemistry from the Hunan University in 2009. Her major research covered chemometrics and nanoarray biosensor. She works as research associate in the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences after graduation. She is mainly working on food safety and inspection technology.

Chunhua Liu received her master's degree in analytical chemistry from Sichuan University in 2007, whose research field was the chromatography and nano technology. Since graduation she has been working as research associate in Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences.
